title: Configuring Uniface Applications to Use TLS
breadcrumbs: Configuring Uniface Applications to Use TLS
text: Configuring Uniface Applications to Use TLS Uniface supports the use of the TLS protocol to secure some or all communication between Uniface servers, clients, databases, and external services such as email and web services. The Uniface TLS connector enables you to use certificate-based public-private key pairs to encrypt communication between communication peers. Example: Configure TLS with a Self-Signed Certificate The following steps demonstrate how to configure a desktop Uniface application to use TLS with a self-signed certificate for server verification. (If you are interested in configuring TLS for a web application, see TLS Connections for Uniface Servlets (WRD and SRD) and Configure a TLS Connection for a Uniface Servlet (WRD or SRD) . In this example, the Uniface Router is acting as the TLS server, with the Uniface Server and Uniface client both acting as TLS clients. Certificates and keys are expected to be in UnifaceInstallPath /common/usys . If you put them in a different location, you need to reflect that in your configuration. Generate a self-signed certificate using OpenSSL You can use OpenSSL to generate keys and certificates, or your preferred tools. Note: On Windows, Uniface provides the OpenSSL executable in the /common/bin folder of the Uniface installation. On Linux, it is usually pre-installed. Alternatively, you can obtain it from one of the sites suggested by the OpenSSL Wiki ( https://wiki.openssl.org/index.php/Binaries ). For more information, consult the OpenSSL documentation ( http://www.openssl.org/ ). The following command generates a self-signed certificate called certificate.crt and a private key called privateKey.key in UnifaceInstallPath /common/usys . cd UnifaceInstallPath /common/usys ../bin/openssl req -x509 -sha256 -nodes -days 365 -newkey rsa:2048 -keyout privateKey.key -out certificate.crt -subj /CN=*. DomainName -config=<ConfigPath> Copy where UnifaceInstallPath is the path to your Uniface installation, DomainName is the machine domain name, and ConfigPath is the path to the OpenSSL configuration file. Tip: To check the machine's domain name: Windows: Open the command prompt and enter the command wmic computersystem get domain Unix: Enter the command hostname -d Note: There is an OpenSSL config file template inside the common\bin folder, that is common\bin\openssl.cnf . You can also configure the OpenSSL configuration file path by adding an environment variable as shown here: OPENSSL_CONF= ConfigPath Copy Edit the Uniface Router assignment file ( urouter.asn ) Configure the TLS connector by defining a TLS profile and its associated settings, which specify the type of verification and the certificate and key to use. (The usys logical specifies that they are located in /common/usys ). [NET_SETTINGS]CertProfile verify_server=yes, server_certificate=usys:certificate.crt, server_key=usys:privateKey.key Copy Change $DEFAULT_NET to use TLS: [SETTINGS]$default_net = TLS:+14001:CertProfile||| Copy 14001 —the port number (or any available port number). CertProfile —name of a TLS profile. This can be any name but it must be the same in both the urouter.asn and the assignment files of clients and servers that connect to this Uniface Router. Optional: Define a server to use instead of the default server. For example: [SERVERS]SRV = BinPath /userver.exe /dir= WorkingDir /adm= UnifaceInstallPath /uniface/adm /asn=userver.asn Copy UnifaceBin —path to userver.exe is present. Uniface installation's bin folder can also be used. WorkingDir —your working directory path, or use an existing server UST definition as an example. UnifaceInstallPath —Uniface installation path. Edit the Uniface Server assignment file ( userver.asn ) Configure the TLS connector so the settings match the TLS server (Uniface Router) definitions: [NET_SETTINGS]CertProfile verify_server=yes, ca_certificate=usys:certificate.crt Copy Edit the desktop client assignment file Configure the TLS connector so the settings match the TLS server (Uniface Router) definitions: [NET_SETTINGS]CertProfile verify_server=yes, ca_certificate=usys:certificate.crt Copy Change the desired path definitions to use the TLS connector with the desired profile. For example: [PATHS]$DEF TLS:+14001:CertProfile| UserverUser | UserverPassword Copy Restart the Uniface Router, Uniface Server, and client application. It should now work as before, but the communication channels are now encrypted. Figure 1.
